prompt:
Our ops tools run on a trimmed-down python image where argparse is one of the modules that got stripped (don't ask), and the hand-rolled sys.argv slicing that grew in its place is now five subtly different dialects. I want ONE in-house engine, argkit.py, that we own end to end. Hard requirement: no argparse, no optparse, no getopt -- the tests actually verify none of those modules get imported. And nothing in argkit may ever touch sys.argv or call sys.exit: the caller passes prog and the argv list in, and bad input comes back as a structured exception we can catch in-process (these parsers also run inside long-lived daemons).

API: `Parser(prog, description=None, add_help=True)`. `add_argument(...)` does positionals and options: a name without dashes is a required positional (filled in definition order; only kwargs `type`, `help`); the first name of an option must be the long `--name` form, optionally followed by short `-x` aliases, with kwargs `type` (a callable, str by default), `default`, `flag=True` for booleans (no value; defaults False; combining flag with type/default/required is a config error), `required=True` (value options only), `help`. Misconfiguration (duplicate names, no long form, flag+default, required+default, mixing positionals with commands, two names on a positional) raises ValueError at definition time -- loud, not at parse time. `add_command(name, description=None)` returns a child Parser wired as a subcommand (child prog is "<prog> <name>"). `parse(argv)` returns a namespace object with attribute access; dest is the long name minus dashes with '-' -> '_' (--max-rows -> ns.max_rows).

Parse semantics: options and positionals interleave freely; long options take a value as the next token or --name=value; short aliases only as separate tokens (no bundling, no -x=v); a lone `--` ends option parsing, everything after is positional; a repeated option keeps the last value. With commands defined, the first non-option token selects the command, ALL remaining tokens go to the child, and the result merges parent attrs, child attrs, and `ns.command`. Encountering -h/--help raises HelpRequested (attribute .text = that parser's full help) immediately, left to right -- junk before -h still errors first.

Errors raise UsageError with .code, .message, and .usage (that parser's usage line; str(e) is "<usage>\nerror: <message>"). Codes and message templates, exactly: unknown-option "unrecognized option: --x" / missing-value "option --x expects a value" / unexpected-value "option --x does not take a value" (flag given =value) / bad-value "invalid <type.__name__> for <--opt or positional name>: '<raw>'" / missing-argument "missing required argument: <name>" / extra-argument "unexpected extra argument: '<tok>'" / missing-option "missing required option: --x" / unknown-command "unknown command: '<tok>'" / missing-command "missing command". After the scan, missing positionals are reported before missing required options, each in definition order.

Help text is part of the contract and the tests pin it byte for byte, so here is the layout, no terminal-width games, no wrapping: format_usage() is one line, "usage: <prog>" then each option in definition order (--help first when add_help) as "[--name]" for flags, "[--name METAVAR]" optional / "--name METAVAR" required for value options (METAVAR = dest uppercased), then positionals by name, then "<command> ..." if commands exist. format_help() = usage, then description, then "arguments:" (positionals), then "commands:", then "options:" -- each section only if non-empty, exactly one blank line between blocks, and it ends with a single newline. Section rows: two-space indent, the invocation column left-justified to the longest invocation in that section, two spaces, then the help text (rows with no help text get no padding). Option invocations look like "--format, -f FORMAT" (long form first, aliases after, metavar last; flags and --help have no metavar). Optional value options with a non-None default get " (default: <value>)" appended to their help. The automatic --help/-h entry reads "show this help message and exit" and sorts first in options.

test_argkit.py is the acceptance contract -- `python3 test_argkit.py` green, test file stays as-is. Stdlib only (minus the three parsing modules above, obviously).
